blocks.body: B1/B2 Grammar Traps gathers short but frequent traps with tenses, modal structures, conditionals, linkers, and inversion.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"At B1/B2, mistakes are often no longer basic. They become combined: you need to track time reference, the form after a modal, the type of clause, or the word order used for emphasis at the same time.","rules":[{"label":"TENSE LOGIC","color":"emerald","text":"Do not mix the conditional type, the time reference, and the verb form: If I knew..., If I had known..., and I wish I had... do different jobs.","example":"If I knew the answer, I would tell you."},{"label":"MODAL PERFECTS","color":"blue","text":"When you talk about deduction, regret, or criticism about the past, you often need the pattern modal + have + V3.","example":"She must have left already."},{"label":"INVERSION AND LINKERS","color":"amber","text":"After some adverbs and formal linkers, not only the meaning but also the grammar changes: Rarely do I..., Although he was tired....","example":"Rarely do we see such a clear example."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Where Learners Most Often Get Lost
blocks.body:
At this level, a mistake is rarely one-dimensional.

  • In conditionals, learners often mix the form after if with the result form: If I would know...
  • In modal perfects, they drop have + V3 and build a mixed form: must went, should went.
  • In linkers, they confuse conjunctions that introduce a clause with structures that need a noun phrase or gerund: although vs despite.
  • In inversion, they forget the auxiliary after a limiting adverb: Rarely I see...

blocks.heading: 2. How the Correct Choice Works
blocks.body:
When you see a more complex sentence, check four things.

  1. What time reference and degree of reality do I mean? -> present imagination, past regret, real result.
  2. Do I need a modal + have + V3 structure? This often appears in deduction, regret, and criticism about the past.
  3. Is the word after the linker a clause or a phrase? Although + clause, but despite + noun / gerund.
  4. Is there a trigger for inversion? After rarely, never, hardly, only then in a more formal style, you usually need the auxiliary before the subject.

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Putting would after if in a normal Second Conditional.","wrong":"If I would know, I would help.","right":"If I knew, I would help.","hint":"In a normal Second Conditional, the if-clause needs the Past Simple, while would stays in the result clause."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Building a modal perfect without have + V3.","wrong":"She must went home.","right":"She must have gone home.","hint":"For deduction about the past, use the pattern modal + have + past participle."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Mixing up although and despite.","wrong":"Despite he was tired, he finished the report.","right":"Although he was tired, he finished the report.","hint":"Although introduces a full clause. After despite, you need a noun phrase or gerund: Despite being tired..."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Forgetting inversion after a limiting adverb.","wrong":"Rarely I see such clear feedback.","right":"Rarely do I see such clear feedback.","hint":"After rarely, never, seldom, hardly in a formal style, you often need the auxiliary before the subject."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick takeaways","items":["In more advanced sentences, check the whole pattern, not only one word.","For past deduction, regret, or criticism, you often need modal + have + V3.","Separate clause linkers from phrase linkers: although does not behave like despite.","After words such as rarely, never, hardly, remember possible inversion in a more formal style."]}
